1: Dibrê ʾāgûr ben-yāqeh hammassāʾ = “The words of Agur son of Jakeh. The oracle (massāʾ).”> Massāʾ is both “burden” and “oracle/utterance” — the technical term for prophetic pronouncements (Isa 13:1; Nah 1:1). Agur and Jakeh do not appear elsewhere in the OT; the names and the following verses suggest a non-Israelite sage of recognized wisdom, or a pseudonymous framework. The speaker claims to have addressed ‘Ithiel’ and ‘Ucal’ (persons or place names debated). The oracle framework authorizes what follows as more than personal musing.
2: Kî baʿar ʾānōkî mêʾîš = “Surely I am more brutish (baʿar, like a beast/animal) than any man” — baʿar (animal/beast) as a self-descriptor: the radical self-deprecation of the sage who has reached the limits of wisdom and acknowledges the gap. Wĕlō' bînat ʾādām lî = “and I do not have the understanding of a human being.”> This is not false modesty but the apophatic starting point: wisdom begins with acknowledging what one does not and cannot know by human means alone.
3: Wĕlō'-lāmadtî ḥokmāh = “I have not learned wisdom” — Agur's disclaimer intensifies: not only brutish but untaught. Wĕdaʿat qĕdōšîm ʾēdāʿ = “nor have I knowledge (dāʿat) of the Holy One (qĕdōšîm).”> Qĕdōšîm (plural) applied to God — either an intensive plural of majesty (the uniquely holy One) or acknowledging the heavenly council; the MKT “Holy One” (singular) follows the most common interpretation. Yādaʿ (H3045, dispute_level=2) here = intimate relational knowledge, not merely propositional awareness.
4: Mî ʿālāh-šāmayim wayyērēd = “Who has ascended to heaven and come back down?”> — the rhetorical questions of vv4-5 are among the most theologically charged in Proverbs. The sequence — ascend heaven, gather wind in fists, wrap waters in garment, establish earth's ends — describes divine transcendence beyond all human capacity. Mah-šĕmô ûmah-šem-bĕnô = “What is his name, and what is his son's name (šem-bĕnô)?”> — the mention of a “son” alongside the Creator God is unique in the OT wisdom corpus; it generates Christological reflection in the Christian tradition (Heb 1:2-3 applies creation-language to the Son). The verse acknowledges that the answer to its own question exceeds human wisdom.
5: Kol-ʾimrat ʾĕlôah ṣĕrûpāh = “Every word of God (ʾĕlôah) proves true (ṣārap, is refined/tested)” — ṣārap (refine/smelt/test) used of divine speech: as silver is refined of dross in the crucible (Ps 12:6), so every divine utterance is pure of error. Māgēn hûʾ laḥōsîm bô = “he is a shield to those who take refuge in him.”> Confidence in the reliability of divine speech produces the trust that finds YHWH as shield — the epistemological trustworthiness of God's word is the foundation of personal refuge.
6: ʾAl-tôsēp ʿal-dĕbārāyw = “Do not add to his words (dĕbārāyw)” — the prohibition against supplementing divine speech with human invention echoes the Deuteronomic prohibition (Deut 4:2; 12:32). Pen-yôkîaḥ bĕkā wĕnikzabĕtā = “lest he rebuke you and you be found a liar.”> The addition of human words to divine speech invites divine rebuke — the human additions are measured against the pure divine word (v5) and found to be the lies they are.
7: Šĕtayim šāʾaltî mêʾittāk = “Two things I ask of you; do not deny them to me before I die” — the prayer formula that introduces vv8-9: a numbered-list prayer of specific requests. Agur's prayer is notable for its concrete, non-triumphalist character — asking not for greatness but for moral safety. The double petition is framed by mortality awareness: “before I die.”>
8: Šāwĕʾ ûdbar kāzāb harḥēq mimmennî = “Keep falsehood and lies (šāwĕʾ ûdbar kāzāb) far from me” — šāwĕʾ (emptiness/vanity/falsehood) and kāzāb (lie/deception) — the prayer to be kept from both empty vanity and deliberate lying. Rēš wĕʿōšer ʾal-tittĕn-lî = “give me neither poverty nor riches (ʿōšer).”> The prayer for the middle way — the rare wisdom petition that refuses both extremes, knowing that both carry spiritual risk.
9: Pen-ʾeśbaʿ wĕkiḥaštî wĕʾāmartî mî YHWH = “lest I be full and deny you, saying ‘Who is the LORD (YHWH, H3068)?’” — the danger of satiety: fullness produces the arrogant forgetting of God that Deut 8:12-14 warns Israel about. Û-pen-ʾiwwārēš wĕgānabĕtî wĕtāpaśtî šēm ʾĕlōhāy = “or lest I be poor and steal and profane (tāpas) the name of my God (ʾĕlōhāy, H430).”> ʾĕlōhîm (H430) — grammatically plural but taking singular verbs; the name “my God” is personalized. The prayer holds together two theological dangers: prosperity-induced atheism and poverty-induced theft that dishonors the divine name.
10: ʾAl-talsēn ʿebed ʾel-ʾădōnāyw = “Do not slander (lāšan, use the tongue against) a servant to his master” — lāšan is literally “to tongue” someone — to use speech as an instrument of harm. Pen-yĕqallĕlekā wĕʾāšāmĕtā = “lest he curse you and you be found guilty.”> The curse of the slandered servant (perhaps invoking divine justice) rebounds on the slanderer; interfering in the master-servant relationship carries its own accountability.
11: Dôr ʾābîw yĕqallēl = “There is a generation that curses its father” — the first of four ‘generation’ (dôr) portraits (vv11-14), each describing a type of moral failure. The father-cursing generation inverts the fifth commandment (Exod 20:12); wĕʾet-ʾimmô lō'-yĕbārēk = “and does not bless its mother.”> The double failure — active cursing of the father, passive withholding of blessing from the mother — covers both poles of parental dishonor.
12: Dôr ṭāhôr bĕ'ênāyw = “There is a generation that is pure in its own eyes” — the self-deception of the morally filthy who believe themselves clean. Ûmĕṣōʾātô lō' ruḥāṣ = “but has not been washed from its filth.”> The purification language (rāḥaṣ, wash) points to the ritual-purity framework: the generation that has never submitted to cleansing stands before God as genuinely dirty despite its own assessment. The verse anticipates the prophetic critique of self-righteous religion (Isa 1:16; Jer 2:22).
13: Dôr māh-rāmû ʿênāyw = “There is a generation — how lofty are their eyes (rāmû ʿênāyw)!”> — rûm ʿênayim (high/lifted eyes) is the idiom for arrogant disdain; the eyes that look down because they are looking down from a superior position. Wĕʿapʿappāyw yinnāśeʾû = “how high their eyelids are lifted!”> The eyelids (ʿapʿappayim, the fluttering eyelids) are another idiom for contemptuous staring. The generation portrait is one of physical pride-posturing that expresses inner arrogance.
14: Dôr ḥărābôt šinnāyw = “There is a generation whose teeth are swords” — the fourth and most violent generation: their teeth are swords and their fangs are knives, devouring the poor and needy from the earth. The eating-as-violence metaphor makes social oppression visceral: the powerful consume the weak the way predatory animals devour prey. Leʾăkōl ʿăniyyîm mêʾereṣ wĕʾebyônîm mêʾādām = “to devour the poor from the earth and the needy from among men.”>
15: Laʿălûqāh šĕtê bānôt hav hav = “The leech has two daughters: ‘Give’ and ‘Give’” — the leech (ʿalûqāh, a hapax legomenon; the blood-sucking worm or vampire) as an image of insatiable demand. The transition to numerical sayings: “Three things are never satisfied; four never say ‘Enough.’” The numerical formula (X+1) is characteristic of Agur's style (cf. vv18,21,24,29) — it creates anticipation and climax.
16: Šĕʾôl wĕʾōṣer reḥem = “Sheol, the barren womb, the earth that is never satisfied with water, and the fire” — the four things never satisfied: Sheol's appetite for the dead, the barren womb's longing for children, the earth's thirst that cannot be permanently quenched, and fire's insatiable consumption. The combination moves from death to fertility to earth to fire — the full range of natural forces that exceed human capacity to fill or satisfy.
17: ʿAyin tileʿag lĕʾāb = “The eye that mocks (lāʿag) a father and scorns to obey a mother” — the eye as the instrument of contempt (linking back to vv13-14). Wĕyiqrûhā ʿōrbê-naḥal wĕyōʾklûhā bĕnê-nāšer = “will be picked out by the ravens of the valley and eaten by the vultures.”> The graphic imagery of unburied death: the eye that showed contempt for parents will be plucked out and devoured by carrion birds — the ultimate reversal of honor and the completion of shame.
18: Šĕlōšāh hēmmāh niplĕʾû mimmennî = “Three things are too wonderful (niplāʾ) for me; four I do not understand (yādaʿ, H3045, dispute_level=2)”> — yādaʿ here = comprehend/master. The “wonderful” (pālāʾ) is the same root as God's works being “wonders” (Ps 77:11); Agur applies the vocabulary of divine mystery to natural phenomena. The four wonders are presented as things that exceed his understanding despite their visibility.
19: Derek hāneśer baššāmāyim = “the way of an eagle in the sky” — the four wonders: eagle in sky, serpent on rock, ship in sea, and man with young woman. What they share is the leaving of no trace: the eagle, snake, and ship pass through their medium without permanent mark; the way of a man with a maiden is similarly impenetrable in its movement. The fourth (“the way of a man with a young woman”) gives the series its climax and its interpretive key: the wonder of intimate relationship is as mysterious as natural phenomena.
20: Kēn derek ʾiššāh mĕnāʾāpet = “Such is the way of an adulteress” — the transition from wonder to warning: the adulteress mimics the wonder of the man-with-maiden passage but with moral inversion. ʾākĕlāh ûmāḥătāh pîhā wĕʾāmĕrāh lō'-pāʿaltî ʾāwen = “she eats and wipes her mouth and says, ‘I have done nothing wrong.’”> The wiping of the mouth erases the trace, exactly as the four wonders left no trace — but here the trace-erasure is self-serving denial of moral reality.
21: Taḥat šālôš rāgĕzāh ʾereṣ = “Under three things the earth trembles (rāgaz); under four it cannot bear up (śāʾ)” — the second four-part series in Agur's oracle, this time with negative-social focus. The earth's trembling is the physical response to the inversion of proper social order; the cosmos reacts to the violation of its built-in hierarchies.
22: Taḥat ʿebed kî yimlôk = “a servant when he reigns” — the first of four social inversions: servant become king. Wĕnābāl kî śābaʿ lāḥem = “and a fool when he is full of food.”> Nābāl (fool/knave) — the morally deficient person. The earth trembles not at the events themselves but at the disproportionate results they produce: a satiated fool is more dangerous than a hungry one.
23: Taḥat śĕnûʾāh kî tibbaʿēl = “an unloved woman when she gets a husband” — the third inversion: the socially overlooked woman who suddenly gains the position of wife and uses it for vengeance or domination. Wĕšipḥāh kî-tîrāš gĕbirtāh = “and a maidservant who displaces (yāraš, inherit/dispossess) her mistress.”> The servant who inherits the mistress's position — the final inversion of domestic order. All four earth-trembling situations involve the abrupt reversal of social position without the formation of character to match.
24: ʾArbaʿāh hēm qĕṭannê-ʾāreṣ = “Four things on earth are small (qāṭan), but they are exceedingly wise (mĕḥukkāmîm mĕʾōd)” — the third four-part series, this time celebratory: smallness with wisdom exceeds greatness without it. The four creatures will demonstrate that size and strength are not the measures of true wisdom.
25: Hanĕmālîm ʿam lō'-ʿāz = “the ants are a people not strong (ʿāz), yet they prepare their food in the summer” — the ant as the paradigm of foresight-wisdom: they plan for winter during summer abundance. The ant of 6:6-8 reappears; here in the context of small-but-wise creatures it demonstrates that wisdom-planning compensates for physical weakness.
26: Šĕpannîm ʿam lō'-ʿāṣûm = “the rock badgers are a feeble people (lō'-ʿāṣûm, not powerful), yet they make their homes in the cliffs” — the rock badger (coney/hyrax) compensates for vulnerability with strategic placement: they live in the inaccessible cliffs where predators cannot easily reach. Wisdom exploits environment; the weak creature uses terrain the strong creature ignores.
27: Hāʾarbeh ʾên melek (melek, H4428, dispute_level=2) = “the locusts have no king (melek)” — the absence of hierarchical leadership does not prevent the locusts from marching in perfect formation. Wĕyēṣēʾ ḥōṣēṣ kullô = “yet all of them march out in formation.”> The locust swarm without a king demonstrates that effective collective action can arise from distributed wisdom rather than central authority. The mention of melek is ironic in context: the animal without a king does what kings fail to achieve.
28: Śĕmāmît bĕyādayim tĕtappēś = “the lizard (śĕmāmît, a hapax — likely a small gecko or lizard) you can take in your hands, yet it is found in kings’ (mĕlek, H4428) palaces.”> The smallest and most easily caught creature reaches the highest human institution: the king's palace. The paradox of access: size and power do not determine where one can be found; the lizard's nimbleness takes it where strength cannot.
29: Šĕlōšāh hēmmāh mêṭîbê ṣāʿad = “Three things are majestic in their stride (ṭôb ṣāʿad); four are stately in their going” — the fourth four-part series, this time focused on dignity and bearing rather than wisdom or social inversion. Ṭôb ṣāʿad = good/fine of step; the dignity that communicates authority through movement.
30: Lāyiš gibbôr baббĕhēmāh = “the lion (lāyiš, a rare word for the great/old lion), which is mightiest among beasts and does not turn back before any.”> — lāyiš (only here and Isa 30:6; Job 4:11) is a more intensive lion term than ʾaryēh; the senior or great lion. The lion's dignity is its fearlessness: it does not retreat. The first in the series of stately things.
31: Zarzîr motnayim ʾô-ṭāyiš = “a strutting rooster or a he-goat (ṭāyiš)” — the word zarzîr (hapax; possibly “one girt in the loins”) is traditionally translated “strutting rooster” or “greyhound” (LXX) — an animal with visible physical readiness/bearing. Wĕmelek (melek, H4428, dispute_level=2) qûm ʿimmô = “and a king (melek) with his army arrayed before him.”> The king as the human example of stately bearing — regal authority visible in posture and movement. The series culminates in the king as the human counterpart to the lion.
32: ʾIm-nābaltā bĕhitnaaśēʾ = “If you have been foolish, exalting yourself (hitnaaśēʾ, lifting yourself up)” — the return to moral instruction after the observation sections. Wĕʾim-zammôtā yād lĕpeh = “or if you have devised evil, put your hand over your mouth.”> Yād lĕpeh (hand over the mouth) = the gesture of silencing oneself, stopping before the foolish exaltation or evil device is fully spoken. The gesture is a form of wisdom — catching oneself before words become action.
33: Kî mîṣ ḥālāb yôṣîʾ ḥemʾāh = “For as pressing (mîṣ) milk produces curds and pressing the nose produces blood, so pressing anger produces strife.”> The concluding proverb of the Agur oracle uses the same physical pressure image three times: the pressing/squeezing that produces inevitable products. Churning milk produces butter; pressing the nose produces blood; pressing anger produces strife. The wisdom application: stop pressing the anger before it produces strife, as you would stop before churning becomes spoilage. The oracle closes with this practical instruction for managing conflict.
